1: The heading identifies this as an oracle (massa) — a word meaning both burden and lifting up/utterance. The double meaning is deliberate: the oracle against Babylon is a heavy burden on the prophet to carry, and a heavy word to pronounce. The MKT's oracle is standard; some preserve the weight dimension with burden. Isaiah the son of Amoz adds the prophetic credential — this is not speculative political analysis but divine revelation.
2: Raise a banner on the bare mountain! Lift your voice to them! Wave your hand, so that they may enter the gates of the nobles. The call to arms imagery — banner, voice, hand — is the language of mobilizing an army. The gates of the nobles are Babylon's famous fortified gates. The MKT preserves the military address without identifying the army — the effect is that the call comes before the caller is identified (Isaiah 13:3: the LORD's consecrated troops).
3: I have given orders to my consecrated troops. The LORD speaks in the first person — the divine Commander addressing his mequddashim (consecrated/holy ones). This is not a standard army but a set-apart force — the same word used for the holy articles of the temple. The consecrated warriors become an instrument of divine judgment without themselves being righteous; they are set apart for the purpose of judgment as Babylon was once set apart for the purpose of disciplining Judah.
4: The clamour of kingdoms, of nations massing together! YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged — the LORD mustering the army of battle. The great noise of the mustering armies is described in terms of rumbling, roaring, clamor — all sound imagery that creates an auditory picture of overwhelming force assembling. The MKT's LORD of hosts preserves the military title; the Commander of heaven's armies is raising an earth-shaking force.
5: They are coming from a distant land, from the far end of the heavens — the LORD himself and the weapons of his wrath. YHWH is flagged — the divine name as the one who comes at the head of the instruments of wrath. The geographical scope (far end of the heavens) suggests cosmic rather than merely regional judgment. The Day of the LORD as cosmic event rather than local military campaign is the key claim here.
6: Wail, for the day of the LORD is close at hand — it will come as devastation from the Almighty. YHWH is flagged. The Hebrew sound-play is famous: shod miShaddai yavo — devastation from the Devastator will come. The MKT's devastation from the Almighty preserves the Shaddai title but loses the sound-play — an acceptable trade-off in English. The Day of the LORD (yom YHWH) is its first major occurrence in Isaiah, framing the oracle as eschatological rather than merely historical.
7: At this every hand will go limp and every human heart will melt with fear. The physical effects of divine judgment — limp hands, melting hearts — are the opposite of the courage described in Isaiah 9:4 (the yoke of burden broken, the rod of the oppressor). When the LORD comes in judgment on the oppressor, even the oppressor's strength fails. The MKT's go limp and melt with fear preserve the bodily images of collapse.
8: They will writhe like a woman in labor. Birth-pang imagery for the suffering of divine judgment — the same image Jesus uses for the beginning of eschatological woes (Matthew 24:8: all these are the beginning of birth pains) and Paul uses for creation's groaning (Romans 8:22). The MKT's labor imagery is consistent with NT use; the pangs of judgment become the pangs of the new creation being born.
9: YHWH is flagged — the Day of the LORD described as relentless in wrath and burning anger. The day arrives with the purposefulness of a determined traveler; it cannot be deflected by the wicked's plans or preparations. The dual goal is stated: to turn the land into a desolation and to banish its sinners from it. Desolation of the land and banishment of sinners are the two coordinates of divine judgment in Isaiah's pattern.
10: The stars of heaven and their constellations will not give their light; the sun will be darkened when it rises, and the moon will not shine. The cosmic darkness of the Day of the LORD — astronomical signs that the ordering of creation is being shaken. Jesus cites the same cluster of images for the end of the age (Matthew 24:29 citing Isaiah 13:10; also Joel 2:10-11; Amos 8:9). The MKT preserves the astronomical specifics (stars, constellations, sun, moon) without reduction to metaphor.
11: I will punish the world for its evil and the wicked for their guilt. The scope expands: the oracle against Babylon becomes an oracle against the world (tevel). Babylon is the world-empire; its judgment types the judgment of every world-empire that sets itself against God. The MKT's world (not earth alone) captures the universal scope of tevel — the inhabited world in its totality.
12: Human life will become rarer than pure gold — rarer even than the gold of Ophir. The scarcity of survivors expressed through the most precious metals. Ophir's gold was the gold of temple-construction quality (1 Kings 10:11) — the finest known. Human beings will become more rare than even this superlative standard of value. The MKT's human life (not merely man) is a good universalizing rendering.
13: YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged — the cosmic shaking is an act of the LORD of hosts. The heavens tremble, the earth is displaced from its foundations. This language of cosmic de-creation (undoing the ordering of Gen 1) appears in NT apocalyptic (Hebrews 12:26-27 citing Haggai 2:6; Revelation 6:12-17). The MKT's tremble and displaced from its foundations are literal and accurate.
14: Like a hunted gazelle or like sheep scattered with no one to round them up. The individual turns to his own people and flees to his own land — the cosmopolitan empire dissolves into ethnic flight. The sheep-without-a-shepherd image (Matthew 9:36; Mark 6:34) resonates here: when the shepherd-king of Babylon fails, the flock scatters. The MKT's double simile (gazelle + sheep) preserves the Hebrew parallelism.
15: Anyone who is caught will be run through with a sword; anyone taken prisoner will fall by the sword. The comprehensive violence of conquest — caught or captured, the outcome is the same. The rhetoric of no escape is deliberate: this is divine judgment executed through human agents, and its thoroughness is the point. No ransom, no escape, no exception for the powerful.
16: Their children will be dashed to pieces in front of them. One of the most disturbing verses in the OT — and one that Psalms 137:9 reflects in reverse (the imprecation against Babylon). The practice was historical and documented in ancient warfare; Isaiah describes it not approvingly but as the horror that will befall Babylon what Babylon inflicted on others. The MKT preserves this without softening, which is important for honest engagement with the text's historical realism.
17: I am rousing the Medes against Babylon — a people who care nothing for silver and feel no delight in gold. The Medes as divine instrument are identified before they are aware of their appointment — the same pattern as Assyria in chapter 10. The Medes' indifference to treasure means they will not be bought off; they are a pure instrument of destruction. Historically, the Medes and Persians under Cyrus conquered Babylon in 539 BC.
18: Their bows will cut the young men down; they will show no mercy on newborn infants. The reversal of the Babylonian victimization of Israel (2 Kings 24-25) by exactly the same methods. The oracle operates on the lex talionis principle applied at the national level: what Babylon did to others will be done to Babylon. The MKT's bows will cut down is literal — arrows cutting through the young men.
19: Babylon, the most glorious of kingdoms, the pride and glory of the Chaldeans, will be overthrown by God like Sodom and Gomorrah. The Elohim title is flagged — the grammatically plural divine name with singular reference. The comparison to Sodom and Gomorrah is the ultimate standard of divine judgment: sudden, total, irreversible. Revelation 18:2 applies this oracle to spiritual Babylon (Rome). The MKT's God renders Elohim as singular, following standard convention.
20: It will never be inhabited or lived in through all generations. The permanent desolation of Babylon is the sign of complete divine judgment. Historically, Babylon declined over centuries but was not immediately or completely desolated after 539 BC — which is why this oracle functions typologically in Revelation as describing the eschatological end of the world-empire principle that Babylon represents, not merely the historical city.
21: Wild animals will rest there, and its houses will be full of howling creatures. The reversal of Babylon's splendor into a haunt for wilderness animals — jackals, ostriches, wild goats, hyenas — is the standard prophetic image of complete urban collapse (also Jer 50-51). The list of animals in the MKT follows the Hebrew; some are difficult to identify precisely (lilith in Isaiah 13:22 is rendered night creature or screech owl). The MKT's howling creatures captures the desolation-ambience.
22: Her time is almost here and her days will not be prolonged. The oracle closes with the urgency of near fulfillment — almost here (karov). The temporal urgency of prophetic oracles operates on both historical and eschatological levels: historically the fall of Babylon was imminent for Isaiah's audience two centuries later; eschatologically the fall of the world-empire system is always near for those who live under its oppression. Revelation 1:3 opens with the same temporal urgency: the time is near.
